definition: A fully managed serverless platform for running stateless containers that automatically scales based on request volume, including scaling to zero.
context: Best choice for HTTP-driven or event-driven stateless workloads with unpredictable traffic.

definition: A GKE mode of operation where Google manages node provisioning, scaling, and most operational aspects of the cluster, billing based on Pod resource requests rather than node capacity.
context: Contrast with GKE Standard, where you manage node pools yourself.

term: Agent Runtime on Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform
definition: Google Cloud's fully managed platform for deploying and running AI agents, providing managed session state, tool orchestration, and multi-step task execution. Formerly named Vertex AI Agent Engine.
context: Purpose-built for conversational, tool-using AI agents — not a general compute platform.

definition: Compute Engine instances offered at up to 91% discount versus standard pricing, suitable for fault-tolerant, interruptible workloads. Formerly called preemptible VMs.
context: Google can reclaim Spot VM capacity at any time with short notice.

definition: Block storage attached to a Compute Engine VM that persists independently of the VM lifecycle and exists within a single zone.
context: Contrast with Regional Persistent Disk (replicated across two zones) and Local SSD (ephemeral, node-local).

definition: A block storage option that allows provisioning IOPS and throughput independently of disk capacity, enabling very high performance for demanding workloads.
context: Useful when you need high IOPS without needing a correspondingly large disk size.

definition: A fully managed, PostgreSQL-compatible database service built for demanding enterprise workloads requiring high performance, availability, and scale.
context: More performant than Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL for many enterprise transactional and analytical workloads.

definition: A fully managed, horizontally scalable relational database that provides global distribution with strong consistency and ACID transactions.
context: The only Google Cloud relational database that scales writes horizontally across regions.

definition: A fully managed NoSQL wide-column database optimized for very high throughput and low-latency access to massive datasets, often used for time-series and IoT data.
context: Not ACID-compliant in the relational sense; optimized for scale over complex querying.

definition: A fully managed in-memory data store service supporting Redis and Memcached, providing sub-millisecond latency for caching use cases.
context: Data is not durable by default — appropriate for caching, session storage, and other rebuildable data.

definition: Storage tiers (Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive) that trade off cost versus access frequency: Standard for frequent access, Nearline for monthly, Coldline for quarterly, Archive for yearly or less.
context: Choosing the wrong class for your access pattern can lead to higher retrieval costs or unnecessary storage costs.

definition: A managed service for transferring large amounts of data into Cloud Storage from on-premises sources, other cloud providers (S3, Azure Blob), or other Cloud Storage buckets.
context: Avoids the bandwidth and time cost of manual downloads/uploads for large-scale data migration.

definition: Google Cloud's modern firewall service supporting hierarchical policies, Secure Tags, and Layer 7 inspection for more granular and centrally managed network security than legacy VPC firewall rules.
context: Can be applied at the organization or folder level for centralized policy enforcement across many VPCs.

definition: Resource Manager tags with IAM-controlled creation and assignment, used as a tamper-resistant alternative to legacy network tags for targeting Cloud NGFW firewall rules.
context: More secure than legacy network tags because only specific IAM roles can create or apply them.

definition: A networking configuration where a host project shares its VPC network with one or more service projects, allowing cross-project resources to communicate using internal IPs.
context: Different from VPC Peering, which connects two separate VPC networks rather than sharing one.

definition: A service providing dedicated physical or partner-based connectivity between an on-premises network and Google Cloud, bypassing the public internet.
context: Offers more consistent bandwidth and lower latency than Cloud VPN, which still traverses the public internet.

definition: A network tier that routes traffic over Google's private global backbone for the lowest latency and highest reliability, entering and exiting Google's network as close to the user as possible.
context: Contrast with Standard Tier, which routes over the public internet and costs less but offers less consistent performance.

definition: Google Cloud's opinionated, Terraform-based foundation toolkit (Fast Architecture Setup Toolkit) for setting up enterprise-grade landing zones with security baselines and hub-and-spoke networking.
context: Accelerates enterprise Google Cloud foundation setup compared to writing Terraform from scratch.

definition: A Kubernetes operator that lets you manage Google Cloud resources as Kubernetes Custom Resources, enabling GitOps-style infrastructure management via kubectl.
context: Different from Terraform — Config Connector uses Kubernetes CRDs rather than HCL.

definition: An AI-powered command-line tool that assists with generating, explaining, and refining infrastructure code and configurations (including Terraform) through natural language.
context: A code-generation and assistance tool, not a resource provisioning tool itself.

definition: A service that routes events from Google Cloud sources (Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, Audit Logs, etc.) to event-driven targets like Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, and GKE as standardized CloudEvents.
context: Commonly used to trigger Cloud Run services when a file lands in Cloud Storage.

definition: Google's custom-designed ASIC optimized for the matrix multiplication operations central to training and running large neural networks, particularly with TensorFlow and JAX.
context: Generally offers higher throughput per dollar than GPUs for large-scale TensorFlow/JAX training.

definition: A fully managed parallel distributed file system designed for HPC workloads requiring extremely high bandwidth and low latency across thousands of compute nodes.
context: Built on the Lustre filesystem widely used in supercomputing environments.

term: Google Cloud Managed Service for Apache Kafka
definition: A fully managed Apache Kafka offering for teams needing Kafka-specific features like topic partitioning, consumer groups, and Kafka Streams for streaming and event-driven architectures.
context: Different from Pub/Sub — Kafka has persistent logs and consumer offset semantics that Pub/Sub does not replicate exactly.

definition: A feature that ties SSH access to Compute Engine VMs to Google IAM identities, enabling centralized access management and automatic revocation when IAM permissions change.
context: Removes the need to manage individual SSH keys per VM.
